page.title: Clean Code & SOLID Principles
page.description: Examine variables naming, function design, and the object-oriented SOLID rules.

sections.title: Introduction to Clean Code
sections.content: Clean code is reader-focused code. As Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob) states, the ratio of time spent reading code versus writing code is over **10 to 1**. Writing readable code is essential for maintainability, velocity, and collaboration.

items.heading: Reveal Intention
items.content: Choose names that tell you why a variable exists, what it does, and how it is used. Avoid abbreviations (e.g., use `elapsedTimeInDays` instead of `d`).

items.heading: Functions Should Do One Thing
items.content: A function should perform exactly one task, do it well, and do it exclusively. If a function contains steps that are at different levels of abstraction, extract them into smaller functions.

items.heading: Avoid Side Effects
items.content: A side effect occurs when a function modifies state outside its local scope (e.g., modifying global state or class properties unexpectedly). This increases coupling and bugs.

tabs.label: S & O
tabs.content:
**Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)**: A class should have one, and only one, reason to change.

**Open/Closed Principle (OCP)**: Software entities should be open for extension, but closed for modification.

tabs.label: L & I
tabs.content:
**Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)**: Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types without altering correctness.

**Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)**: Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use.

tabs.label: D
tabs.content: **Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)**: High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions. Abstractions should not depend on details; details should depend on abstractions.

questions.question: Which principle states that a class should have only one reason to change?
questions.explanation: The Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) mandates that a class must have only one focus or responsibility, meaning there is only one source of change.
optionExplanations: Correct! SRP focuses on single responsibility and source of change.
OCP focuses on extension without modifying code.
DIP focuses on depending on abstractions.

questions.question: If a method in a subclass throws an UnsupportedOperationException when substituting its parent class, which principle is violated?
questions.explanation: This violates the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP) because you cannot replace instances of the superclass with the subclass without breaking the system behaviour.
optionExplanations: SRP does not deal directly with subclass substitution.
Correct! LSP requires subtypes to be completely interchangeable with parent types.
ISP deals with splitting large interfaces.
DIP deals with programming to interfaces instead of implementations.
